mnemonics: I.S.E. for Interrupt State Enable: Remember the order of operations - Interrupts are often disabled automatically during Service, and must be Explicitly re-enabled afterwards.
keyPoints: Interrupts are typically disabled automatically by the processor when an interrupt service routine (ISR) begins execution to prevent nested interrupts and ensure data integrity.

The programmer must explicitly re-enable interrupts before the end of the ISR, often using a specific instruction like 'EI' (Enable Interrupt) or by popping the original status register value from the stack.

Global interrupt enable/disable flags (like the I flag in status registers) control the entire interrupt system, while mask registers can be used to selectively enable/disable specific interrupt sources.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to re-enable interrupts before exiting an ISR, which can leave the system unresponsive to future interrupt requests.
Enabling interrupts too early within an ISR, before critical data has been saved or processed, leading to corrupted data from nested interrupts.
Confusing the global interrupt enable flag with individual interrupt mask bits, believing that setting a mask enables the interrupt when it typically disables it.
